text: We describe how structural equation modeling can be used to detect
response shifts and to measure true change.
Here we will describe another structural
equation modeling procedure to detect recalibra-
tion, reprioritization, and reconceptualization re-
sponse shifts, using data from two measurement
occasions only (e.g., pretest-posttest data or
baseline-followup data). With this procedure it is
not only possible to detect various types of re-
sponse shifts, but also to account for them and to
still measure true change.
In structural equation modeling we model mean
and covariance structures, rather than the scores
of individual subjects, as in Equation (1).
When a test or questionnaire is administered at
two occasions, the parameter matrices that feature
in Equations (2) and (3) are made up of smaller
matrices.
That is, with Table 1 we submit that an
across occasion difference within the parameter
matrix in the first column is indicative of the type
of change in the third column, which can be tested
with the invariance hypothesis in the fourth
column.
Various types of change, including response shifts
and true change, can be investigated by testing the
across occasion invariance of (elements of) the
submatrices given by Equations (5) through (7).
If the value of the factor loading of a particular
observed variable changes from one occasion to
the next, then that variable has become more
indicative or less indicative of the concept in-
volved.

pattern of the F1 matrix differs from pattern of the
F2 matrix, then one or more concepts are defined
by unequal sets of observed variables, and we
conclude that reconceptualization occurred.

In the model given by Equation (1), the
common factors 4 can be seen as operationaliza-
tions of target constructs, so that the change in the
common factor means cx can be used as the oper-
ationalization of true change.
Table 2 summarizes the four steps in a procedure
for the detection of response shifts and the mea-
surement of true change. In this procedure, struc-
tural equation modeling is used to investigate the
invariance hypotheses listed in Table 1.
The difference in the
X2 measures of fit of Models 1 and 2 can thus be
used as an overall test of no response shift. If
That is, common factor
loadings that change from zero into non-zero (or
vice versa) indicate reconceptualization, other
changes in common factor loadings indicate rep-
rioritization, changes in intercepts indicate uni-
form recalibration, and changes in residual
variances indicate non-uniform recalibration.
In
Model 3, with all response shifts accounted for, the
change in common factor means, represented by
X2, is a valid measure of true change.
where the first component, (T2 - TI ),constitutes
the contribution of recalibration, the second
component, (F2 - F)OC2, constitutes the contribu-
tion of reconceptualization and reprioritization,
and the third component, F l2, constitutes
the contribution of true change to observed
change.
The procedure for the detection of response shifts
and true change uses structural equation modeling
of means and covariances.
So in this example
(with hypothetical true values for all model
parameters), we would need around 200 subjects
to detect a reprioritization response shift, which is
indicated by the difference between two non-zero
factor loadings.
For example, a reconceptualization or
reprioritization response shift, reflected in a
stronger (or weaker) effect of a latent variable
(e.g., general mental health) on an observed vari-
able (e.g., SF-36 scales such as GH or SF), appears
in the covariance structure (through factor load-
ings) only if a substantial number of individuals
experience that response shift.
Schmitt [16, 17] considers the
covariance structure only, applying a model as
given by Equation (3), thus disregarding types of
change that appear in the mean structure, such as
uniform recalibration and true change. Millsap
and Hartog [18] apply a model for both covariance
and mean structures, similar to Equations (2) and
(3), and reparameterize it as a regression model.
So, although Schmitt and Millsap and Hartog
also apply structural equation modeling to inves-
As a result, changes in intercepts reflect
changes in the group means of the specific factors,
and changes in residual variances primarily reflect
changes in the variances of the specific factors.
tigate different types of change, their operational-
izations and detection procedures are different
from ours.
In an at-
tempt to solve the confusion caused by the mix of
change typologies, definitions, interpretations,
operationalizations, and detection procedures in
the response shift literature [2-4], the psychometric
approach yields clear definitions, interpretations,
and operationalizations of changes in measure-
ment characteristics and changes in subject char-
acteristics.
Most of these changes, but not all, can
be detected through structural equation modeling
of means and covariances observed on two mea-
surement occasions [14].
